abstract: Agent skills, structured packages of procedural knowledge and executable resources that agents dynamically load at inference time, have become a reliable mechanism for augmenting LLM agents. Yet inference-time skill augmentation is fundamentally limited: retrieval noise introduces irrelevant guidance, injected skill content imposes substantial token overhead, and the model never truly acquires the knowledge it merely follows. We ask whether skills can instead be internalized into model parameters, enabling zero-shot autonomous behavior without any runtime skill retrieval. We introduce SKILL0, an in-context reinforcement learning framework designed for skill internalization. SKILL0 introduces a training-time curriculum that begins with full skill context and progressively withdraws it. Skills are grouped offline by category and rendered with interaction history into a compact visual context, teaching he model tool invocation and multi-turn task completion. A Dynamic Curriculum then evaluates each skill file's on-policy helpfulness, retaining only those from which the current policy still benefits within a linearly decaying budget, until the agent operates in a fully zero-shot setting. Extensive agentic experiments demonstrate that SKILL0 achieves substantial improvements over the standard RL baseline (+9.7\% for ALFWorld and +6.6\% for Search-QA), while maintaining a highly efficient context of fewer than 0.5k tokens per step. Our code is available at this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: Peng Xia, Jianwen Chen, Hanyang Wang, Jiaqi Liu, Kaide Zeng, Yu Wang, Siwei Han, Yiyang Zhou, Xujiang Zhao, Haifeng Chen, et al. Skillrl: Evolving agents via recursive skill-augmented reinforcement learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.08234, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper introduces SkillRL, a key baseline representing the 'skill augmentation' paradigm where skills are provided in-context during inference. SKILL0 directly contrasts its 'skill internalization' approach with SkillRL's, uses it as a primary performance benchmark, and even initializes its skill library from SkillRL's resources, making it a fundamental point of comparison.
citations.title: Skillrl: Evolving agents via recursive skill-augmented reinforcement learning

citations.fullCitation: Lang Feng, Fuchao Yang, Feng Chen, Xin Cheng, Haiyang Xu, Zhenglin Wan, Ming Yan, and Bo An. Agentocr: Reimagining agent history via optical self-compression. arXiv preprint arXiv:2601.04786, 2026.
citations.justification: This work is the direct inspiration for SKILL0's context rendering mechanism, which compresses textual history and skills into an image to reduce token overhead. AgentOCR also serves as a critical reinforcement learning baseline in the experiments, allowing for a direct comparison of SKILL0's internalization approach against another method that uses a compressed visual context.
citations.title: Agentocr: Reimagining agent history via optical self-compression

citations.fullCitation: Renjun Xu and Yang Yan. Agent skills for large language models: Architecture, acquisition, security, and the path forward. arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.12430, 2026.
citations.justification: This paper is cited as a foundational work that defines and surveys the concept of 'agent skills' as reusable, structured knowledge. SKILL0 builds directly upon this concept by proposing a novel method for skill acquisition (internalization), positioning its contribution within the broader research area framed by this work.
citations.title: Agent skills for large language models: Architecture, acquisition, security, and the path forward

citations.fullCitation: Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, and Shunyu Yao. Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning, 2023. URL https://arxiv. org/abs/2303.11366, 8, 2024.
citations.justification: Reflexion is a seminal work in enhancing agent capabilities through self-reflection and experience, a form of verbal reinforcement learning. It is included as a key prompt-based agentic baseline in the extended comparisons, representing an alternative approach to improving agent performance without direct skill injection, thereby providing a valuable reference point for SKILL0's learning-based internalization method.
citations.title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning

intermediateReport:
This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "SKILL0: In-Context Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Skill Internalization," focusing on its contributions to the field of large language model (LLM) agents.

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors primarily affiliated with Zhejiang University and Meituan, with one author from Tsinghua University.

*   **Zhengxi Lu:** Zhejiang University, Meituan
*   **Zhiyuan Yao:** Meituan
*   **Jinyang Wu:** Tsinghua University
*   **Chengcheng Han:** Meituan
*   **Qi Gu:** Meituan
*   **Xunliang Cai:** Meituan
*   **Weiming Lu:** Zhejiang University
*   **Jun Xiao:** Zhejiang University
*   **Yueting Zhuang:** Zhejiang University
*   **Yongliang Shen:** Zhejiang University

### 2. How this work fits into the broader research landscape

The development of LLM agents capable of complex real-world tasks has been significantly advanced by the concept of Agent Skills. These skills are structured units of procedural knowledge and executable resources that LLM agents dynamically access during inference. The prevailing paradigm for leveraging these skills is "inference-time skill augmentation," where relevant skills are retrieved from a skill bank and injected into the model's context as natural language guidance at each step of an interaction. This approach has led to the establishment of skill libraries, retrieval pipelines, and evolution mechanisms, proving effective in extending agent capabilities.

However, the authors identify several fundamental limitations with inference-time skill augmentation:
1.  **Retrieval Noise:** The retrieval process can introduce irrelevant or misleading guidance, which corrupts the agent's contextual understanding.
2.  **Token Overhead:** Injected skill content adds to the token count within the context window, a cost that accumulates across multi-turn interactions and can limit scalability.
3.  **Lack of Internalization:** Critically, a model that merely follows skill descriptions in its prompt is executing skills rather than truly acquiring or internalizing the underlying knowledge. The competence resides in the context provided, not within the model's parameters, making the agent perpetually dependent on external guidance.

This paper positions itself as a response to these limitations, moving beyond skill *augmentation* to skill *internalization*. It draws an analogy to human skill acquisition, which progresses from explicit instruction to an internalized phase where behavior is executed autonomously from memory. The work argues that current methods keep agents anchored in the explicit instruction phase. While reinforcement learning (RL) offers a path to internalizing strategies, a naive application faces challenges: without skill context, agents lack guidance for complex tasks; with full skill context, they remain externally dependent. SKILL0 aims to bridge this gap by introducing a training regime designed to systematically transfer competence from the external context into the model's parameters.

The related work section further contextualizes SKILL0 by reviewing advancements in LLM Agents, including their application in various environments and the emergence of agentic RL for robust decision-making. It also discusses Agentic Skills, noting how previous research primarily focused on skill extraction, organization, and retrieval from external databases. SKILL0 differentiates itself by explicitly addressing the "largely unexplored" question of whether skills can be internalized into model parameters, rather than just managed externally.

### 3. Key objectives and motivation

The primary objective of this research is to develop a framework that enables LLM agents to internalize skills directly into their model parameters, thereby eliminating the need for runtime skill retrieval and achieving zero-shot autonomous behavior at inference time. This is encapsulated by the motto: "Skills at training, zero at inference."

The motivation for this objective stems from the inherent limitations of the prevalent inference-time skill augmentation paradigm, as detailed in the previous section. The authors critically observe that existing methods, while functional, result in models that merely *follow* instructions rather than *learn* them. This dependency on external context at every inference step leads to:
*   **Inefficiency:** High token costs associated with injecting skill descriptions into the prompt, which compound in multi-turn interactions.
*   **Fragility:** Susceptibility to retrieval noise, where irrelevant or incorrect skills can degrade performance.
*   **Lack of True Agency:** The agent's competence remains external, preventing the development of intrinsic decision-making capabilities. The model never truly "acquires the knowledge it merely follows."

To address these issues, the authors pose a fundamental question: "rather than asking how to better retrieve and inject skills, can skills be internalized into model parameters, rendering retrieval unnecessary at inference time?" This question drives the core motivation: to transition LLM agents from a state of context-dependent execution to one of autonomous, memory-based behavior, akin to how humans internalize procedural knowledge.

SKILL0 is thus motivated to introduce a training paradigm that starts with the explicit guidance of skills during training and systematically withdraws this guidance as the model learns, ultimately enabling the agent to operate without any external skill context during inference. This involves formulating skill internalization as an explicit training objective within an RL framework.

### 4. Methodology and approach

SKILL0 introduces an In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) framework designed for skill internalization through a training-time curriculum. The methodology consists of three main components: the Agent Loop, In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL), and Adaptive Curriculum Learning.

#### 4.1. Agent Loop
The agent automation is framed as a sequential decision-making problem where an agent generates actions ($a_t$) based on task instructions ($I$) and interaction history ($h_t$).

*   **Skill Management:** Instead of dynamic retrieval of individual skills based on semantic similarity, SKILL0 organizes reusable behavioral knowledge into a hierarchical `SkillBank`. This `SkillBank` contains General skills (universal principles) and Task-specific skills (domain-specific knowledge), stored in Markdown files ($S_k$) grouped by task and category. During training, a subset $S$ of these skill files is selected based on an "on-policy helpfulness" criterion, which estimates their utility to the current policy. This moves away from noisy retrieval to a more structured, training-driven selection process.

*   **Context Rendering:** To address token costs, SKILL0 maps the textual interaction context (history $h_t$ and selected skills $S$) into a compact RGB image $V_t$ using a vision encoder ($Enc$). This visual representation significantly reduces token overhead while preserving structural information. The policy also learns to dynamically generate a compression ratio ($c_t$) at each step, alongside the task action, allowing for adaptive context compression.

#### 4.2. In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL)
ICRL combines the benefits of skill prompting (for structured guidance and sample efficiency) with reinforcement learning (for exploration and policy optimization).

*   **Skill Provision during Training:** Skills are provided as in-context guidance during training rollouts, but entirely removed during inference. This design ensures that RL optimization directly targets the internalization of skills into model parameters.

*   **Composite Reward:** To incentivize both task success and efficient context compression, a composite reward ($r_{comp,t}$) is introduced. This reward combines a binary success indicator ($I_{succ}(\tau)$) with a logarithmic penalty based on the compression ratio ($c_t$). The total reward ($\tilde{r}_t$) includes the task-specific reward ($r_t$) and the composite reward, weighted by $\lambda$, balancing performance and efficiency.

*   **Training Objective:** SKILL0 utilizes a PPO-like objective function ($L_{SKILL0}(\theta)$). This objective optimizes the policy based on importance-sampled advantage values ($A_i$) derived from the composite rewards. It also includes a KL divergence regularization term to prevent large policy shifts from a reference model ($\pi_{ref}$).

#### 4.3. Adaptive Curriculum Learning
A crucial aspect of SKILL0 is its adaptive curriculum, which progressively reduces the agent's reliance on external skills to encourage internalization. This process operates in two phases:

*   **Relevance-Driven Skill Grouping (Offline):** Prior to training, the validation set (a subset of the training data) is partitioned into $N$ sub-tasks ($T_k$). Each $T_k$ groups instances whose skill requirements align with a specific skill file $S_k$. This offline grouping creates dedicated validation tasks for evaluating the utility of each skill file.

*   **Helpfulness-Driven Dynamic Curriculum (Online):** The training is divided into $N_S$ progressive stages, each with a linearly decaying skill budget ($M^{(s)}$). This budget gradually limits the number of active skill files available to the agent.
    *   **Helpfulness Evaluation:** Every `d` training steps, the helpfulness metric ($\Delta_k$) for each skill file $S_k$ is computed. This is done by comparing the policy's performance on its associated validation sub-task $T_k$ *with* $S_k$ provided versus *without* $S_k$.
    *   **Filter & Rank & Select:** For each stage $s$, the skill files ($S_k$) are filtered to retain only those with positive helpfulness ($\Delta_k > 0$). These beneficial skills are then ranked in descending order by their $\Delta_k$ values. Finally, the top-$M^{(s)}$ skills are selected to form the active skill subset for the current training stage. If $M^{(s)}$ becomes zero, no skills are provided.
    *   This dynamic filtering and selection mechanism ensures that only skills still beneficial to the current policy are retained, progressively forcing the model to internalize knowledge as its competence grows. The linear decay of the budget ensures a smooth transition to a fully self-reliant state.

### 5. Main findings and results

The experiments demonstrate SKILL0's effectiveness in skill internalization, achieving improved performance and efficiency across diverse tasks.

#### 5.1. Method Performance
*   **Superiority over RL Baselines:** SKILL0 consistently outperforms strong RL baselines like AgentOCR. On ALFWorld, SKILL0 (3B model) achieves an average success rate of 87.9%, a +9.7% improvement over AgentOCR (78.2%). On Search-QA, it reaches 40.8%, a +6.6% improvement over AgentOCR (34.2%). With 7B models, SKILL0 further improves to 89.8% on ALFWorld and 44.4% on Search-QA, surpassing other RL-based methods such as EvolveR, AgentOCR, and GRPO.
*   **Competitive with Skill-Augmented Methods:** Despite eliminating external skill reliance at inference, SKILL0 achieves performance competitive with or even stronger than skill-augmented methods like SkillRL (e.g., 87.9% vs. 82.4% on ALFWorld 3B, and 40.8% vs. 38.9% on Search-QA 3B).
*   **Broader Comparisons:** Extended comparisons in supplementary tables show SKILL0 largely outperforms memory-augmented learning methods (e.g., ExpeL, Mem0, MemRL) on ALFWorld, and retrieval-augmented/search-based methods (e.g., Search-R1, ZeroSearch, EvolveR) on Search-QA. It also surpasses several closed-source models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) on ALFWorld. Notably, SKILL0 shows robust generalization on out-of-domain multi-hop datasets like Bamboogle (63.7%/66.9%).

#### 5.2. Token Efficiency
*   SKILL0 demonstrates significantly lower context token costs. Using 3B models, it consumes only 0.38k tokens per step on ALFWorld and 0.18k on Search-QA. This represents a substantial reduction (over 5x) compared to text-based or skill-augmented methods like SkillRL, which incur 2.21k and 0.87k tokens per step, respectively. This efficiency is attributed to visual context modeling and skill internalization, removing the need for explicit skill descriptions in the prompt at inference time.

#### 5.3. Training Dynamics
*   **Reward Curves:** SKILL0 maintains consistently higher reward curves throughout RL optimization compared to the AgentOCR baseline for both 3B and 7B models, indicating more effective learning.
*   **Skill Internalization Trend:** Validation accuracy tracked during training reveals that models evaluated *with* skill augmentation achieve faster early-stage performance improvement. However, models validated *without* skill prompts, while starting lower, gradually catch up and often surpass skill-augmented variants by the end of optimization. This pattern provides empirical evidence of skill internalization.
*   **Skill-Free Inference:** In a strictly fair comparison setting (no skill prompts at inference), SKILL0 outperforms AgentOCR, confirming its performance advantage stems from internalized knowledge. It also continues to improve steadily throughout optimization, achieving higher performance upper bounds compared to text-based RL baselines (GRPO, SkillRL) under skill-free inference.
*   **Helpfulness Metric ($\Delta_k$):** The helpfulness of skills exhibits a "rise-then-fall" pattern across sub-tasks. Initially low, it increases as the policy learns to leverage skill prompts, and then converges towards zero in later stages as the dynamic curriculum reduces the skill budget, compelling internalization. This trajectory validates the synergistic effect of ICRL and curriculum learning, where skills act as transient scaffolding.

#### 5.4. Ablation Studies
*   **Skill Budget M:** Comparing different skill budgets, the dynamic linear decay budget (e.g., [6, 3, 0] for ALFWorld) demonstrates superior skill internalization. While fixed high budgets (e.g., [6, 6, 6]) collapse significantly when skills are removed (-13.3%), SKILL0 with its dynamic budget even achieves a slight gain (+1.6%) in skill-free inference, indicating successful internalization and reduced over-reliance. Static low budgets ([3, 3, 3]) limit early exploration and lead to unstable learning.
*   **Dynamic Curriculum (Filter & Rank & Select):** All three components of the helpfulness-driven strategy are crucial. Removing the "Filter" step (using all skills up to the budget) leads to a performance drop (-2.7%) due to context noise. Removing the "Rank" step (randomly selecting skills) causes a severe collapse (-13.7%), emphasizing the necessity of retaining only demonstrably helpful skills for stable policy learning and preventing superficial prompt dependency.
*   **Validation Interval d:** An interval of d=10 for helpfulness evaluation was chosen as an optimal trade-off between task performance and computational overhead, with smaller intervals offering marginal gains at higher cost.

### 6. Significance and potential impact

SKILL0 makes a significant contribution by introducing and validating skill internalization as a "principled alternative" to the retrieve-then-prompt paradigm for LLM agents. The primary significance lies in demonstrating that agents can move beyond mere execution of external instructions to truly *learn* and *internalize* complex procedural knowledge and tool-use behaviors into their core model parameters.

The potential impacts are multifaceted:

*   **Enhanced Autonomy:** By enabling zero-shot autonomous behavior without runtime skill retrieval, SKILL0 paves the way for LLM agents that are truly self-sufficient and less dependent on external, potentially noisy, guidance. This moves the field towards "truly autonomous LLM agents and self-sufficient intelligence."
*   **Improved Efficiency:** The substantial reduction in context token cost (over 5x compared to some baselines) during inference is a practical benefit. This efficiency translates to lower computational resource usage and faster inference times, making LLM agents more scalable and economically viable for real-world applications.
*   **Robustness and Reliability:** Internalizing skills can reduce susceptibility to retrieval noise and errors, making agents more robust in dynamic or uncertain environments where perfect skill retrieval might not always be possible.
*   **More Profound Learning:** The framework encourages a deeper form of learning in LLM agents, where competence becomes intrinsic rather than residing solely in the prompt. This could lead to more generalizable and adaptable agents.
*   **New Research Direction:** SKILL0 establishes skill internalization as a new research paradigm, inviting further exploration into how LLMs can acquire and consolidate knowledge for enhanced agency.

**Limitations:** The authors acknowledge that SKILL0's reliance on the quality of the initial `SkillBank` is a limitation. Furthermore, the offline relevance-driven skill grouping mechanism requires re-partitioning when applied to entirely new task domains, indicating that the initial setup phase might not be fully automated for novel environments.
overview:
In the rapidly evolving field of Large Language Model (LLM) agents, a key goal is to develop systems that can perform complex, multi-step tasks autonomously. Currently, the most successful approach involves "skill augmentation," where an agent is given a library of procedural knowledge—such as how to use a specific tool or how to solve a logic puzzle—and retrieves relevant skills to include in its prompt at each step. While effective, this creates a perpetual dependency: the agent never actually "learns" the skill; it simply follows the instructions provided in its context window.

SKILL0 proposes a shift from this "retrieve-and-prompt" paradigm toward **skill internalization**. Drawing an analogy to human learning, where we move from following a manual to performing a task from memory, SKILL0 introduces a framework that allows agents to consolidate external skills into their own model parameters. The core philosophy is "Skills at training, zero at inference," meaning that while the agent uses skills as scaffolding during its learning phase, it eventually operates entirely without them.

![Comparison of Skill Augmentation vs. Skill Internalization](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02268v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Traditional skill augmentation (top) relies on continuous retrieval from a skill bank, increasing token costs and noise. SKILL0 (bottom) uses a training-time curriculum to internalize these skills, enabling zero-shot inference without external prompts.*

## The Challenges of Skill Augmentation

Current LLM agents primarily rely on inference-time augmentation, where a skill bank is searched for relevant textual descriptions that are then injected into the model's prompt. While this allows models to handle tasks they weren't originally trained for, it introduces several fundamental bottlenecks:

1.  **Retrieval Noise:** Semantic search (finding skills based on text similarity) is imperfect. Irrelevant or misleading skill descriptions can confuse the agent, leading to "context corruption" where the model fails because it follows the wrong advice.
2.  **Token Overhead:** Injecting long skill descriptions significantly increases the number of tokens the model must process. In multi-turn interactions, these costs compound, making the system slower and more expensive to run.
3.  **Lack of True Agency:** If an agent can only perform a task when a manual is provided in its context, it hasn't truly acquired the capability. The intelligence resides in the prompt, not the model parameters. This makes the agent fragile—if the retrieval system fails, the agent's performance collapses.

SKILL0 addresses these issues by treating skills as a curriculum for Reinforcement Learning (RL). By providing skills during training but systematically withdrawing them, the model is forced to internalize the logic of those skills into its weights.

## The SKILL0 Technical Framework

The SKILL0 architecture is designed to handle the transition from guided execution to autonomous mastery. It consists of three primary layers: a visual-aware agent loop, an in-context reinforcement learning objective, and an adaptive curriculum.

![SKILL0 Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02268v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The SKILL0 framework, showing the interaction between the skill bank, the visual agent loop, and the curriculum-driven reinforcement learning process.*

### 1. The Agent Loop and Visual Rendering
The agent operates in a sequential environment, generating actions $a_t$ based on a task instruction $I$ and the history of interactions $h_t$. To solve the token overhead problem, SKILL0 uses a vision-based approach. Rather than feeding long text histories back into the model, the system renders the textual context into a compact RGB image $V_t$ using a vision encoder $Enc$. This visual representation preserves the structure of the task history while using far fewer tokens than raw text.

Furthermore, the model learns to output a dynamic compression ratio $c_t$. This allows the agent to decide how much detail it needs from its history at any given time, further optimizing efficiency.

### 2. Hierarchical SkillBank
Instead of flat retrieval, SKILL0 organizes knowledge into a `SkillBank` containing General skills (logic, general tool-use) and Task-specific skills. During training, the agent doesn't just retrieve based on similarity; it selects a subset $S$ of skill files based on their "on-policy helpfulness"—a measure of how much a specific skill actually improves the current model's performance on a task.

## In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL)

The internalization process is driven by a specialized reinforcement learning objective. Standard RL often struggles with "sparse rewards," where an agent gets no feedback until the very end of a long task. By providing skills in the context during training, SKILL0 gives the agent "scaffolding" that makes early exploration more successful.

To encourage both task success and efficiency, SKILL0 utilizes a composite reward function $\tilde{r}_t$:

$$\tilde{r}_t = r_t + \lambda (I_{succ}(\tau) - \log(c_t))$$

In this equation, $r_t$ is the standard task reward, $I_{succ}(\tau)$ is a binary indicator of whether the task was ultimately successful, and $\log(c_t)$ is a penalty for high context usage. The hyperparameter $\lambda$ balances the trade-off between succeeding at the task and being efficient with memory.

The policy is updated using a PPO-like objective function $L_{SKILL0}(\theta)$. This objective uses importance-sampled advantage values $A_i$ to update the model weights. To ensure the model doesn't deviate too drastically from its original reasoning capabilities, a KL divergence regularization term is included, which penalizes the difference between the current policy and a stable reference model $\pi_{ref}$.

## Adaptive Curriculum Learning

The most critical component of SKILL0 is the **Helpfulness-Driven Dynamic Curriculum**. This is what ensures that skills are actually internalized rather than just followed. The curriculum operates in two phases:

### Phase 1: Relevance-Driven Grouping (Offline)
Before training begins, the system partitions the training data into $N$ sub-tasks $T_k$. Each sub-task is associated with specific skill files $S_k$ from the SkillBank. This creates a structured environment where the system knows which skills are theoretically relevant to which tasks.

### Phase 2: Dynamic Decay (Online)
Training is divided into $N_S$ stages. In each stage $s$, the agent has a "skill budget" $M^{(s)}$—the maximum number of skill files it can look at. Critically, this budget $M^{(s)}$ decays linearly over time, eventually reaching zero.

To decide which skills to keep as the budget shrinks, the system evaluates the **helpfulness** $\Delta_k$ of each skill. Every $d$ steps, the model is tested on a validation sub-task $T_k$ both with and without the skill $S_k$. If the model performs just as well without the skill, $\Delta_k$ is low, and the skill is a candidate for removal. If the model still struggles without it, the skill is kept in the prompt to provide continued guidance.

This "rise-then-fall" pattern of helpfulness is a hallmark of internalization:
*   **Early Training:** The agent hasn't learned the skill, so providing it in the prompt helps significantly (high $\Delta_k$).
*   **Late Training:** The agent has "memorized" the skill logic into its parameters, so the prompt becomes redundant (low $\Delta_k$).

## Experimental Results and Performance

SKILL0 was evaluated on two challenging benchmarks: **ALFWorld** (a synthetic environment for multi-step household tasks) and **Search-QA** (complex question answering requiring multi-hop search).

### Performance and Efficiency
The results demonstrate that SKILL0 significantly outperforms existing RL-based agents. On ALFWorld, a 3B-parameter model using SKILL0 achieved an 87.9% success rate, outperforming the baseline AgentOCR (78.2%). More importantly, it achieved this while using significantly fewer tokens.

| Method | Success Rate (ALFWorld) | Tokens per Step |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SkillRL (Augmentation) | 82.4% | 2.21k |
| AgentOCR (Baseline) | 78.2% | 0.81k |
| **SKILL0 (Ours)** | **87.9%** | **0.38k** |

As shown in the table, SKILL0 is not only more effective but also over 5 times more token-efficient than standard skill-augmented methods. This efficiency stems from the fact that at inference time, SKILL0 does not need to retrieve or process any skill descriptions.

### Training Dynamics
The training curves provide evidence of the internalization process. Initially, models evaluated with skill prompts perform better. however, as training progresses, the "skill-free" performance of SKILL0 catches up to and eventually matches the "skill-guided" performance.

![Reward Curves during RL Training](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02268v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: Reward curves for 3B models showing SKILL0 (red) maintaining higher and more stable rewards than the baseline (blue).*

![Internalization Accuracy](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02268v1/x5.png)
*Figure 4: Accuracy tracked during training. Note how the "without skill" accuracy (purple) eventually converges with the "with skill" accuracy (green), signifying successful internalization.*

## Ablation: Why the Curriculum Matters

The researchers conducted ablation studies to determine if the decaying budget was truly necessary. They compared the decaying budget $[6, 3, 0]$ with a fixed high budget $[6, 6, 6]$.

![Ablation Study of Skill Budgets](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.02268v1/ablations.png)
*Figure 5: Comparison of different budget strategies. The decaying budget (left) is the only one that achieves a performance gain when skills are removed, indicating successful internalization.*

If the model is always given skills during training (fixed high budget), it becomes "lazy" and over-reliant on the prompt. When those skills are removed at inference time, performance drops by over 13%. In contrast, SKILL0's decaying curriculum forces the model to take over the reasoning process, leading to a slight performance *increase* when the "distracting" prompts are finally removed at the end of training.

## Conclusion

SKILL0 represents a significant step toward creating truly autonomous AI agents. By moving beyond the "retrieve-then-prompt" model, it demonstrates that complex procedural knowledge can be consolidated directly into the parameters of a large language model. This not only makes agents more efficient and less expensive to operate but also makes them more robust.

The success of the helpfulness-driven curriculum suggests that "scaffolding" is a powerful tool for agentic reinforcement learning. By providing the right information at the right time—and then taking it away—we can train agents that don't just follow instructions but truly "understand" the skills required to navigate complex environments. While the framework currently depends on a high-quality initial skill bank, it sets the stage for future research into agents that can autonomously discover, refine, and internalize their own libraries of expertise.

keyInsights: Skill internalization is a viable and advantageous alternative to inference-time skill augmentation, allowing LLM agents to transition from context-dependent execution to truly autonomous, memory-based behavior.

A dynamically decaying skill budget, combined with filtering and ranking skills by their 'helpfulness,' is crucial for successful internalization, preventing agents from over-relying on external prompts.

Providing skills as transient scaffolding during training, and then gradually withdrawing them, effectively guides the agent to consolidate knowledge within its parameters, leading to superior skill-free inference performance.
originalProblem: Existing inference-time skill augmentation methods suffer from retrieval noise, where irrelevant or misleading guidance can corrupt the agent's contextual understanding.
Injected skill content creates high token overhead in the context window, leading to increased computational costs and limiting scalability in multi-turn interactions.
Current LLM agents merely follow explicit skill descriptions, remaining perpetually dependent on external guidance rather than truly internalizing knowledge into their model parameters.
results: SKILL0 achieved superior performance, with a 3B model reaching an 87.9% success rate on ALFWorld (+9.7% over AgentOCR) and 40.8% on Search-QA (+6.6%), demonstrating its ability to internalize skills effectively.
The framework significantly reduced context token costs during inference, consuming over 5 times fewer tokens per step compared to text-based or skill-augmented methods like SkillRL.

Validation accuracy tracked during training provided empirical evidence of skill internalization, as models evaluated without skill prompts eventually surpassed skill-augmented variants by the end of optimization.
solution: SKILL0 employs an In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) framework where skills are provided as in-context guidance during training, but are entirely removed during inference to promote internalization.

An Adaptive Curriculum Learning mechanism progressively reduces the active skill budget and dynamically selects beneficial skills based on a 'helpfulness' metric, compelling agents to internalize knowledge over time.

The system utilizes visual context modeling to map textual interaction history and skills into compact RGB images, significantly reducing token overhead, alongside a composite reward incentivizing both task success and context compression.
summary.summary: The SKILL0 framework introduces In-Context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) to enable Large Language Model (LLM) agents to internalize skills into their parameters, thereby achieving autonomous behavior without external skill descriptions at inference time. This approach yielded superior performance, such as an 87.9% success rate on ALFWorld (+9.7% over AgentOCR), while substantially reducing context token costs by over 5 times compared to skill-augmented methods.

title: SKILL0: In-Context Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Skill Internalization
